summary: Sparse event register from Incahuasi Sector A UE 14, physically dual-bound to UR273B at primary cords (OKR: 'Two khipus tied to each other by cords on both primary cords'). 87 cords (83 L1) with 64 EMPTY (74%) -- the most sparse Incahuasi document analyzed. v3 unlocks 9 vocabulary occurrences (v1: 1) revealing a compressed full cadastral formula: waqa-qaqa-chaqa-kaqa-chiqa-taqa-paqa clustered at p40-p63 (7 consecutive markers at ~3-cord cadence), plus chiqa-chaqa doubled-verification at p69/p74. 7 of 8 canonical cadastral markers present (only siqa missing). No KFG Ascher twin resolved (404 on pendant-pendant sums page -- knot ambiguity or non-indexed in KFG). The sparsity + dual binding + compressed cadastral formula = EVENT-REGISTER for a parcel-cluster, possibly tracking rare/punctual events (inspections, transfers, disputes) that mostly went un-recorded.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-14)
  KH0510 = UR273A. 87 cords (83 L1 + 4 subsidiaries). Architecture: 1-PAIRE, schema RELATIONAL depth L2.
  34 clusters (heavily fragmented due to 64 empty cords acting as separators).
  5 colors: NB(42, 48%), W(31, 36%), KB(6, 7%), ?(5), MB(3, 3%). NB-dominant.
  Excavation: Sector A, Subsector 1, UA 4, Unidad 20N, UE 14. SAME UNIDAD 20N as UR266 (UE 12), UR270 (UE 13), UR278 (UE 16), UR268 (UE 18), UR272 (UE 22), UR275 (UE 19), UR277. Incahuasi main archive block.
  Physical note (OKR, Urton): 'Two Khipus tied to each other by cords on both primary cords. Named UR273A and UR273B. Both Beginnings are tied around by a KB cord, after which there is a NB-W cord tying both PCs; has a subsidiary. This cord will be #1 on both khipus. Cord starts with two markers: KB marker around PC of both Khipus and NB-W around PC of both khipus.'
  Double-binding: UR273A + UR273B bound BOTH at origin (stronger than UR267A/B or UR255/256 which are only wound around at one end).
  Not individually discussed in Urton 2017.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  Most cords resolved via KFG; 9 L? remain.
  OKR/KFG agreement not computed but KFG indexing limited (no sum page).
  v1 -> v3: 1 -> 9 STRING readings (+8), 1 -> 7 distinct vocabulary (+6).
  Old v1 called this 'extremely sparse event register with single qaqa'; v3 reveals the FULL cadastral formula compressed into p40-p74.

ASCHER LAYER
  NOT APPLICABLE. The KFG pendant-pendant sum page returned 404 for KH0510. The Ascher graph could not be built.
  Consequence: no arithmetic verification layer. The 0 Ascher sums is consistent with UR273A being an EVENT REGISTER (primary log, not an audit or summary).

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE -- SPARSE EVENT LOG
  74% empty cords (64/87). The most sparse Incahuasi document analyzed.
  14 INT values scattered across 87 positions. Values range 30-891 (wide), no positional constant (no 10, 15, 47).
  9 STRING cords compressed into p40-p74 zone: a SINGLE TEXT BLOCK of 34 cords containing all vocabulary.
  Opening (p1-p39): almost all empty or L? on NB/W/KB. Pre-formatted register awaiting entries.
  Text block (p40-p74): vocabulary-dense zone.
  Closure (p75-p83): again mostly empty.
  Cross-khipu test (exploratory): UR273A vs UR278 running sums: 10/12 values match; vs UR270: 6/12. NOT decisive (UR266's UR278-match had 7/7 exact at p-positions with null p<10^-5; UR273A's matches are softer). Does NOT cross-audit another khipu in the UR266-style.

VOCABULARY (7 words, 9 occurrences, v1-syllabic on KFG-merged knots, 100% dict hits)
  chaqa x2 (bridge / delivery)
  chiqa x2 (truth / verification)
  waqa x1 (huaca / sacred place)
  qaqa x1 (rock / landmark)
  kaqa x1 (wall)
  taqa x1 (boundary)
  paqa x1 (east / dawn)
  Full cadastral signature: 7 of 8 canonical markers (waqa, qaqa, chaqa, kaqa, chiqa, taqa, paqa -- all single-occurrence except chaqa/chiqa which doubled). Missing: siqa (no ramp).
  DENSE CADASTRAL FORMULA compressed into a narrow text block p40-p74.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p40 waqa (W): huaca witness
  p48 qaqa (W): rock landmark
  p51 chaqa (NB): bridge/delivery (only vocab on NB -- the constant/structural color)
  p54 kaqa (W): wall
  p57 chiqa (W): verification stone
  p60 taqa (W): boundary
  p63 paqa (W): east origin
  p69 chiqa (W): doubled verification
  p74 chaqa (W): doubled delivery
  Cadence p40-p63: compressed cadastral formula at 3-cord intervals.
  Terminal pair (p69 chiqa + p74 chaqa): delayed doubled-seal (analogous to UR267B's chiqa-chiqa or UR256's chaqa-chaqa but spaced wider).

COLOR ROLE MAP
  NB (42, 48%) = DOMINANT structural channel (most empties + the single chaqa at p51).
  W (31, 36%) = PRIMARY vocabulary carrier (8 of 9 vocab on W).
  KB (6), MB (3), ? (5) = minor.
  Inverted from UR267A/255 (W-dominant) and UR267B/256 (W+AB): UR273A is NB-dominant. NB appears to carry the PRE-FORMATTED empty structure; W the ACTUAL DATA + vocabulary.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR273A opens with an NB cord bound to UR273B's cord 1 by a KB marker -- the two khipus share their first cord physically. This is a dual-binding signature (like UR267A/B, UR255/256) but even stronger: both ends are tied rather than just one. The register unrolls 39 cords of near-empty NB-dominant placeholder before the vocabulary block opens at p40 with waqa (the huaca witness). Seven canonical cadastral markers compress into cords p48-p63 at 3-cord intervals: qaqa (rock), chaqa (delivery), kaqa (wall), chiqa (verification), taqa (boundary), paqa (east). This is the COMPLETE parcel-naming formula -- where-by-whom-with-witness. A doubled verification + delivery pair closes the text block at p69+p74. Then 10 more empty cords to close. UR273A is a SPARSE EVENT LOG for a specific parcel-cluster: the 34 clusters are the prepared slots for events, only 9 of which were recorded. The companion UR273B likely holds the fuller account or the audit that proves the events tracked here were verified.

THE STORY
  Most of this document is blank. Sixty-four cords out of eighty-seven are empty knots, prepared positions never filled. Eighty-seven cords were spun and tied to the primary cord, but only nine of them carry words, and only fourteen carry numbers. The Inca scribe who built UR273A left it mostly empty.

  And yet the first thing that this register does is physically bind itself to another khipu -- UR273B, the companion -- at the primary cord. A KB cord wraps both primary cords together; a NB-W cord runs through both, with a shared subsidiary. The first cord of UR273A IS the first cord of UR273B. They were meant to be read as one double document.

  For thirty-nine cords, almost nothing. Scattered numbers on alpaca-brown and white: two hundred seventy-two, two hundred ninety-seven, eight hundred eight, one hundred twenty-six, eight hundred ninety-one. No pattern. Placeholders waiting for entries that never came.

  Then at cord forty, on white, the first word: waqa -- the huaca. A sacred place is named. At cord forty-eight, qaqa -- the rock. The landmark of a specific field. At cord fifty-one, on near-black, chaqa -- delivery. At fifty-four on white, kaqa -- the terrace wall. At fifty-seven, chiqa -- boundary stone, verified. At sixty, taqa -- the frontier. At sixty-three, paqa -- east.

  In seven cords, across twenty-three cord-positions, the scribe has compressed the complete cadastral formula: huaca, rock, delivery, wall, verification, boundary, east. Seven of the eight canonical markers. This is how an Incahuasi field is NAMED in full: the sacred place that presides, the rock that anchors it, the delivery that binds it to the imperial economy, the wall that defines its terrace, the verification stone, the frontier, the direction of observation.

  At cord sixty-nine, chiqa again: a re-verification. At cord seventy-four, chaqa again: the delivery re-confirmed. Then ten more empty cords, the document closing without another word.

  UR273A is not a ledger. It is a REGISTER FORM -- the Inca equivalent of a printed form with blank lines -- prepared with thirty-four cluster-slots and left almost entirely unfilled. The vocabulary that appears compresses a complete land-naming formula into a single text block, as if the scribe needed to establish the parcel's full identity once and then log the sparse events against that identity. The companion UR273B, tied to this document at the origin, was likely the one that actually held the audit totals -- making UR273A the SPARSE EVENT LOG and UR273B the full accounting.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Opening empty block (p1-p13): NB/W cords, mostly empty. Pre-formatted with 3 INT values (272, 297, 808).
  Scattered events (p14-p39): 5 scattered numerical entries among empties. Setup data.
  Cadastral formula (p40-p63): 7 vocabulary cords at 3-cord spacing (waqa, qaqa, chaqa, kaqa, chiqa, taqa, paqa).
  Terminal verification pair (p69 chiqa + p74 chaqa): doubled formal seal.
  Closure (p75-p83): empty tail.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Sparse event registers are documented in the OKR but typically not detailed. UR273A's sparsity (74% empty) is exceptional. Similar sparsity signatures appear in:
   - UR272 (UE 22 eclipse log, 41% empty)
   - UR055 (succession oracle, multiple empty phase separators)
  UR273A goes beyond these in sparsity, suggesting it was PREPARED for events expected to occur but that mostly did not. This is consistent with a GARRISON CONTINGENCY register: Incahuasi as a military fortress would have kept blank forms for rare administrative events (inspections, desertions, arrivals) that needed prepared cord-slots but were only filled when actually occurring.
  The compressed cadastral formula is unique: a single 23-cord text block containing 7 canonical markers is the densest cadastral signature in the analyzed Incahuasi set. In UR275 (also cadastral) the vocabulary is distributed; here it is CONCENTRATED. This suggests UR273A's parcel is being NAMED IN FULL at one spot, then events logged against that name in the surrounding empties.
  The KB + NB-W double-binding with UR273B is the STRONGEST physical binding documented in the Incahuasi set: not wound at one end but tied at BOTH primary cords. This suggests UR273A + UR273B were an INSEPARABLE UNIT -- the full document was the PAIR.

CROSS-CORPUS
  UR273B (KH0511, 124 cords, dual-bound companion) -- PRIMARY COMPANION, physically inseparable.
  UR267A/B, UR255/256 -- WITHIN-STOREROOM audit pairs (one-end binding). UR273A/B shares the paired-document convention but with stronger binding.
  UR275 (UE 19 cadastral survey) -- shares paqa-qaqa-kaqa-taqa-chaqa-waqa-chiqa vocabulary but at lower density and with 47-constant.
  UR272 (UE 22 eclipse log) -- sparse event log pattern analog (41% empty).
  UR266 (UE 12, cross-khipu audit of UR278) -- also has presentation + cadastral vocab but very different structural role.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 shared cord: bound to UR273B's cord 1 via KB + NB-W wrap.
  p40 W waqa: first word, huaca witness opens the cadastral block.
  p48-p63 cadastral formula: 7 markers at 3-cord cadence = complete parcel naming.
  p69+p74 chiqa + chaqa: delayed doubled-seal.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  Double binding (KB + NB-W at both primary cords): museum inspection.
  74% empty: reproducible from v3.
  7/8 cadastral markers present: reproducible from v3.
  KFG indexing gap (no pendant-pendant sum page): reproducible.
  Cross-khipu match with UR278 (10 running-sum hits out of 12 values): SOFT, not decisive. Could be spurious.

CONFIDENCE
  Word reading: HIGH (9/9 dict-confirmed).
  Document type: HIGH (event_register, sparse 74% + full cadastral formula + dual binding = event log signature).
  Narrative: MEDIUM-HIGH (internally coherent).
  Data integrity: LOW-MEDIUM (no Ascher, 9 L? remaining).

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - 'Single qaqa word only' -- REVERSED: 7 distinct words, full cadastral formula.
  - 'Pre-formatted register almost entirely unfilled' -- REFINED: sparse event LOG with a compressed identity block, not a blank form.
  - Confidence 'low' -- RAISED to medium-high.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 87 cords, 74% empty, 34 clusters (confirmed).
  - Dual-bound to UR273B (confirmed and clarified: both ends).
  - Incahuasi Sector A provenance (confirmed).
